question:
Let $T$ be the set of all triples $(a,b,c)$ of positive integers for which there exist triangles with side lengths $a,b,c$. Express
\[
\sum_{(a,b,c) \in T} \frac{2^a}{3^b 5^c} 
\]
as a rational number in lowest terms.
solution:
\textbf{First solution:}
The answer is $17/21$. For fixed $b,c$, there is a triangle of side lengths $a,b,c$ if and only if $|b-c|<a<b+c$. It follows that the desired sum is
\[
S = \sum_{b,c} \frac{1}{3^b5^c} \left(\sum_{a=|b-c|+1}^{b+c-1} 2^a \right) = \sum_{b,c} \frac{2^{b+c}-2^{|b-c|+1}}{3^b5^c}.
\]
We write this as $S = S_1+S_2$ where $S_1$ sums over positive integers $b,c$ with $b\leq c$ and $S_2$ sums over $b>c$. Then
\begin{align*}
S_1 &= \sum_{b=1}^\infty \sum_{c=b}^\infty \frac{2^{b+c}-2^{c-b+1}}{3^b 5^c} \\
&= \sum_{b=1}^\infty \left( \left( \left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^b-\frac{2}{6^b} \right) \sum_{c=b}^\infty \left(\frac{2}{5} \right)^c \right) \\
&= \sum_{b=1}^\infty \left( \left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^b-\frac{2}{6^b} \right) \frac{5}{3} \left( \frac{2}{5} \right)^b \\
&= \sum_{b=1}^\infty \left( \frac{5}{3} \left(\frac{4}{15}\right)^b - \frac{10}{3} \left(\frac{1}{15}\right)^b \right) \\
&= \frac{85}{231}.
\end{align*}
Similarly,
\begin{align*}
S_2 &= \sum_{c=1}^\infty \sum_{b=c+1}^\infty \frac{2^{b+c}-2^{b-c+1}}{3^b 5^c} \\
&= \sum_{c=1}^\infty \left( \left( \left(\frac{2}{5}\right)^c-\frac{2}{10^c} \right) \sum_{b=c+1}^\infty \left(\frac{2}{3} \right)^b \right) \\
&= \sum_{c=1}^\infty \left( \left(\frac{2}{5}\right)^c-\frac{2}{10^c} \right) 3 \left( \frac{2}{3} \right)^{c+1} \\
&= \sum_{c=1}^\infty \left( 2 \left(\frac{4}{15}\right)^c - 4 \left(\frac{1}{15}\right)^c \right) \\
&= \frac{34}{77}.
\end{align*}
We conclude that $S = S_1+S_2 = \frac{17}{21}$.

\noindent
\textbf{Second solution:}
Recall that the real numbers $a,b,c$ form the side lengths of a triangle if and only if
\[
s-a, s-b, s-c > 0 \qquad s = \frac{a+b+c}{2},
\]
and that if we put $x = 2(s-a), y = 2(s-b), z = 2(s-c)$,
\[
a = \frac{y+z}{2}, b = \frac{z+x}{2}, c = \frac{x+y}{2}.
\]
To generate all \emph{integer} triples $(a,b,c)$ which form the side lengths of a triangle, we must also assume that $x,y,z$ are either all even or all odd. We may therefore write the original sum as
\[
%\sum_{(a,b,c) \in T} \frac{2^a}{3^b 5^c}
%=
\sum_{x,y,z >0 \mbox{\small \,odd}} \frac{2^{(y+z)/2}}{3^{(z+x)/2} 5^{(x+y)/2}}
+ \sum_{x,y,z >0 \mbox{\small \,even}} \frac{2^{(y+z)/2}}{3^{(z+x)/2} 5^{(x+y)/2}}.
\]
To unify the two sums, we substitute in the first case  $x = 2u+1, y = 2v+1, z = 2w+1$ and in the second case $x = 2u+2, y = 2v+2, z = 2w+2$ to obtain
\begin{align*}
\sum_{(a,b,c) \in T} \frac{2^a}{3^b 5^c}
&= \sum_{u,v,w=1}^\infty \frac{2^{v+w}}{3^{w+u} 5^{u+v}} \left( 1 + \frac{2^{-1}}{3^{-1} 5^{-1}} \right) \\
&= \frac{17}{2} \sum_{u=1}^\infty \left( \frac{1}{15} \right)^u \sum_{v=1}^\infty
\left( \frac{2}{5} \right)^v \sum_{w=1}^\infty \left( \frac{2}{3} \right)^w \\
&= \frac{17}{2} \frac{1/15}{1-1/15} \frac{2/5}{1-2/5} \frac{2/3}{1-2/3} \\
&= \frac{17}{21}.
\end{align*}

descriptive_long.question:
Let $tripleset$ be the set of all triples $(sideone,sidetwo,sidethr)$ of positive integers for which there exist triangles with side lengths $sideone,sidetwo,sidethr$. Express
\[
\sum_{(sideone,sidetwo,sidethr) \in tripleset} \frac{2^{sideone}}{3^{sidetwo} 5^{sidethr}} 
\]
as a rational number in lowest terms.

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Let $cloudberry$ be the set of all triples $(marigold,shoelace,driftwood)$ of positive integers for which there exist triangles with side lengths $marigold,shoelace,driftwood$. Express
\[
\sum_{(marigold,shoelace,driftwood) \in cloudberry} \frac{2^{marigold}}{3^{shoelace} 5^{driftwood}} 
\]
as a rational number in lowest terms.
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
\textbf{First solution:}
The answer is $17/21$. For fixed $shoelace,driftwood$, there is a triangle of side lengths $marigold,shoelace,driftwood$ if and only if $|shoelace-driftwood|<marigold<shoelace+driftwood$. It follows that the desired sum is
\[
undermoon = \sum_{shoelace,driftwood} \frac{1}{3^{shoelace}5^{driftwood}} \left(\sum_{marigold=|shoelace-driftwood|+1}^{shoelace+driftwood-1} 2^{marigold} \right) = \sum_{shoelace,driftwood} \frac{2^{shoelace+driftwood}-2^{|shoelace-driftwood|+1}}{3^{shoelace}5^{driftwood}}.
\]
We write this as $undermoon = undermoon_1+undermoon_2$ where $undermoon_1$ sums over positive integers $shoelace,driftwood$ with $shoelace\leq driftwood$ and $undermoon_2$ sums over $shoelace>driftwood$. Then
\begin{align*}
undermoon_1 &= \sum_{shoelace=1}^\infty \sum_{driftwood=shoelace}^\infty \frac{2^{shoelace+driftwood}-2^{driftwood-shoelace+1}}{3^{shoelace} 5^{driftwood}} \\
&= \sum_{shoelace=1}^\infty \left( \left( \left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^{shoelace}-\frac{2}{6^{shoelace}} \right) \sum_{driftwood=shoelace}^\infty \left(\frac{2}{5} \right)^{driftwood} \right) \\
&= \sum_{shoelace=1}^\infty \left( \left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^{shoelace}-\frac{2}{6^{shoelace}} \right) \frac{5}{3} \left( \frac{2}{5} \right)^{shoelace} \\
&= \sum_{shoelace=1}^\infty \left( \frac{5}{3} \left(\frac{4}{15}\right)^{shoelace} - \frac{10}{3} \left(\frac{1}{15}\right)^{shoelace} \right) \\
&= \frac{85}{231}.
\end{align*}
Similarly,
\begin{align*}
undermoon_2 &= \sum_{driftwood=1}^\infty \sum_{shoelace=driftwood+1}^\infty \frac{2^{shoelace+driftwood}-2^{shoelace-driftwood+1}}{3^{shoelace} 5^{driftwood}} \\
&= \sum_{driftwood=1}^\infty \left( \left( \left(\frac{2}{5}\right)^{driftwood}-\frac{2}{10^{driftwood}} \right) \sum_{shoelace=driftwood+1}^\infty \left(\frac{2}{3} \right)^{shoelace} \right) \\
&= \sum_{driftwood=1}^\infty \left( \left(\frac{2}{5}\right)^{driftwood}-\frac{2}{10^{driftwood}} \right) 3 \left( \frac{2}{3} \right)^{driftwood+1} \\
&= \sum_{driftwood=1}^\infty \left( 2 \left(\frac{4}{15}\right)^{driftwood} - 4 \left(\frac{1}{15}\right)^{driftwood} \right) \\
&= \frac{34}{77}.
\end{align*}
We conclude that $undermoon = undermoon_1+undermoon_2 = \frac{17}{21}$.

\noindent
\textbf{Second solution:}
Recall that the real numbers $marigold,shoelace,driftwood$ form the side lengths of a triangle if and only if
\[
honeycomb-marigold,\; honeycomb-shoelace,\; honeycomb-driftwood > 0 \qquad honeycomb = \frac{marigold+shoelace+driftwood}{2},
\]
and that if we put $playfield = 2(honeycomb-marigold),\; toothpick = 2(honeycomb-shoelace),\; rainspout = 2(honeycomb-driftwood)$,
\[
marigold = \frac{toothpick+rainspout}{2}, \quad shoelace = \frac{rainspout+playfield}{2}, \quad driftwood = \frac{playfield+toothpick}{2}.
\]
To generate all \emph{integer} triples $(marigold,shoelace,driftwood)$ which form the side lengths of a triangle, we must also assume that $playfield,toothpick,rainspout$ are either all even or all odd. We may therefore write the original sum as
\[
%\sum_{(marigold,shoelace,driftwood) \in cloudberry} \frac{2^{marigold}}{3^{shoelace} 5^{driftwood}}
%=
\sum_{playfield,toothpick,rainspout >0 \mbox{\small \,odd}} \frac{2^{(toothpick+rainspout)/2}}{3^{(rainspout+playfield)/2} 5^{(playfield+toothpick)/2}}
+ \sum_{playfield,toothpick,rainspout >0 \mbox{\small \,even}} \frac{2^{(toothpick+rainspout)/2}}{3^{(rainspout+playfield)/2} 5^{(playfield+toothpick)/2}}.
\]
To unify the two sums, we substitute in the first case  $playfield = 2silverfox+1,\; toothpick = 2toadflax+1,\; rainspout = 2dragonfly+1$ and in the second case $playfield = 2silverfox+2,\; toothpick = 2toadflax+2,\; rainspout = 2dragonfly+2$ to obtain
\begin{align*}
\sum_{(marigold,shoelace,driftwood) \in cloudberry} \frac{2^{marigold}}{3^{shoelace} 5^{driftwood}}
&= \sum_{silverfox,toadflax,dragonfly=1}^\infty \frac{2^{toadflax+dragonfly}}{3^{dragonfly+silverfox} 5^{silverfox+toadflax}} \left( 1 + \frac{2^{-1}}{3^{-1} 5^{-1}} \right) \\
&= \frac{17}{2} \sum_{silverfox=1}^\infty \left( \frac{1}{15} \right)^{silverfox} \sum_{toadflax=1}^\infty
\left( \frac{2}{5} \right)^{toadflax} \sum_{dragonfly=1}^\infty \left( \frac{2}{3} \right)^{dragonfly} \\
&= \frac{17}{2} \frac{1/15}{1-1/15} \frac{2/5}{1-2/5} \frac{2/3}{1-2/3} \\
&= \frac{17}{21}.
\end{align*}

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Let $emptycollection$ be the set of all triples $(tininess,narrowness,slimness)$ of positive integers for which there exist triangles with side lengths $tininess,narrowness,slimness$. Express
\[
\sum_{(tininess,narrowness,slimness) \in emptycollection} \frac{2^{tininess}}{3^{narrowness} 5^{slimness}} 
\]
as a rational number in lowest terms.
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
\textbf{First solution:}
The answer is $17/21$. For fixed $narrowness,slimness$, there is a triangle of side lengths $tininess,narrowness,slimness$ if and only if $|narrowness-slimness|<tininess<narrowness+slimness$. It follows that the desired sum is
\[
difference = \sum_{narrowness,slimness} \frac{1}{3^{narrowness}5^{slimness}} \left(\sum_{tininess=|narrowness-slimness|+1}^{narrowness+slimness-1} 2^{tininess} \right) = \sum_{narrowness,slimness} \frac{2^{narrowness+slimness}-2^{|narrowness-slimness|+1}}{3^{narrowness}5^{slimness}}.
\]
We write this as $difference = difference_1+difference_2$ where $difference_1$ sums over positive integers $narrowness,slimness$ with $narrowness\leq slimness$ and $difference_2$ sums over $narrowness>slimness$. Then
\begin{align*}
difference_1 &= \sum_{narrowness=1}^\infty \sum_{slimness=narrowness}^\infty \frac{2^{narrowness+slimness}-2^{slimness-narrowness+1}}{3^{narrowness} 5^{slimness}} \\
&= \sum_{narrowness=1}^\infty \left( \left( \left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^{narrowness}-\frac{2}{6^{narrowness}} \right) \sum_{slimness=narrowness}^\infty \left(\frac{2}{5} \right)^{slimness} \right) \\
&= \sum_{narrowness=1}^\infty \left( \left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^{narrowness}-\frac{2}{6^{narrowness}} \right) \frac{5}{3} \left( \frac{2}{5} \right)^{narrowness} \\
&= \sum_{narrowness=1}^\infty \left( \frac{5}{3} \left(\frac{4}{15}\right)^{narrowness} - \frac{10}{3} \left(\frac{1}{15}\right)^{narrowness} \right) \\
&= \frac{85}{231}.
\end{align*}
Similarly,
\begin{align*}
difference_2 &= \sum_{slimness=1}^\infty \sum_{narrowness=slimness+1}^\infty \frac{2^{narrowness+slimness}-2^{narrowness-slimness+1}}{3^{narrowness} 5^{slimness}} \\
&= \sum_{slimness=1}^\infty \left( \left( \left(\frac{2}{5}\right)^{slimness}-\frac{2}{10^{slimness}} \right) \sum_{narrowness=slimness+1}^\infty \left(\frac{2}{3} \right)^{narrowness} \right) \\
&= \sum_{slimness=1}^\infty \left( \left(\frac{2}{5}\right)^{slimness}-\frac{2}{10^{slimness}} \right) 3 \left( \frac{2}{3} \right)^{slimness+1} \\
&= \sum_{slimness=1}^\infty \left( 2 \left(\frac{4}{15}\right)^{slimness} - 4 \left(\frac{1}{15}\right)^{slimness} \right) \\
&= \frac{34}{77}.
\end{align*}
We conclude that $difference = difference_1+difference_2 = \frac{17}{21}$.

\noindent
\textbf{Second solution:}
Recall that the real numbers $tininess,narrowness,slimness$ form the side lengths of a triangle if and only if
\[
fullperim-tininess, fullperim-narrowness, fullperim-slimness > 0 \qquad fullperim = \frac{tininess+narrowness+slimness}{2},
\]
and that if we put $voidness = 2(fullperim-tininess), emptiness = 2(fullperim-narrowness), nullness = 2(fullperim-slimness)$,
\[
tininess = \frac{emptiness+nullness}{2}, narrowness = \frac{nullness+voidness}{2}, slimness = \frac{voidness+emptiness}{2}.
\]
To generate all \emph{integer} triples $(tininess,narrowness,slimness)$ which form the side lengths of a triangle, we must also assume that $voidness,emptiness,nullness$ are either all even or all odd. We may therefore write the original sum as
\[
%\sum_{(a,b,c) \in T} \frac{2^a}{3^b 5^c}
%=
\sum_{voidness,emptiness,nullness >0 \mbox{\small \,odd}} \frac{2^{(emptiness+nullness)/2}}{3^{(nullness+voidness)/2} 5^{(voidness+emptiness)/2}}
+ \sum_{voidness,emptiness,nullness >0 \mbox{\small \,even}} \frac{2^{(emptiness+nullness)/2}}{3^{(nullness+voidness)/2} 5^{(voidness+emptiness)/2}}.
\]
To unify the two sums, we substitute in the first case  $voidness = 2terminus+1, emptiness = 2endpoint+1, nullness = 2haltings+1$ and in the second case $voidness = 2terminus+2, emptiness = 2endpoint+2, nullness = 2haltings+2$ to obtain
\begin{align*}
\sum_{(tininess,narrowness,slimness) \in emptycollection} \frac{2^{tininess}}{3^{narrowness} 5^{slimness}}
&= \sum_{terminus,endpoint,haltings=1}^\infty \frac{2^{endpoint+haltings}}{3^{haltings+terminus} 5^{terminus+endpoint}} \left( 1 + \frac{2^{-1}}{3^{-1} 5^{-1}} \right) \\
&= \frac{17}{2} \sum_{terminus=1}^\infty \left( \frac{1}{15} \right)^{terminus} \sum_{endpoint=1}^\infty
\left( \frac{2}{5} \right)^{endpoint} \sum_{haltings=1}^\infty \left( \frac{2}{3} \right)^{haltings} \\
&= \frac{17}{2} \frac{1/15}{1-1/15} \frac{2/5}{1-2/5} \frac{2/3}{1-2/3} \\
&= \frac{17}{21}.
\end{align*}

garbled_string.question:
Let $qzxwvtnp$ be the set of all triples $(hjgrksla,mfqlsted,povndyex)$ of positive integers for which there exist triangles with side lengths $hjgrksla,mfqlsted,povndyex$. Express
\[
\sum_{(hjgrksla,mfqlsted,povndyex) \in qzxwvtnp} \frac{2^{hjgrksla}}{3^{mfqlsted} 5^{povndyex}} 
\]
as a rational number in lowest terms.
garbled_string.solution:
\textbf{First solution:}
The answer is $17/21$. For fixed $mfqlsted,povndyex$, there is a triangle of side lengths $hjgrksla,mfqlsted,povndyex$ if and only if $|mfqlsted-povndyex|<hjgrksla<mfqlsted+povndyex$. It follows that the desired sum is
\[
lkjshdwe = \sum_{mfqlsted,povndyex} \frac{1}{3^{mfqlsted}5^{povndyex}} \left(\sum_{hjgrksla=|mfqlsted-povndyex|+1}^{mfqlsted+povndyex-1} 2^{hjgrksla} \right) = \sum_{mfqlsted,povndyex} \frac{2^{mfqlsted+povndyex}-2^{|mfqlsted-povndyex|+1}}{3^{mfqlsted}5^{povndyex}}.
\]
We write this as $lkjshdwe = lkjshdwe_1+lkjshdwe_2$ where $lkjshdwe_1$ sums over positive integers $mfqlsted,povndyex$ with $mfqlsted\leq povndyex$ and $lkjshdwe_2$ sums over $mfqlsted>povndyex$. Then
\begin{align*}
lkjshdwe_1 &= \sum_{mfqlsted=1}^\infty \sum_{povndyex=mfqlsted}^\infty \frac{2^{mfqlsted+povndyex}-2^{povndyex-mfqlsted+1}}{3^{mfqlsted} 5^{povndyex}} \\
&= \sum_{mfqlsted=1}^\infty \left( \left( \left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^{mfqlsted}-\frac{2}{6^{mfqlsted}} \right) \sum_{povndyex=mfqlsted}^\infty \left(\frac{2}{5} \right)^{povndyex} \right) \\
&= \sum_{mfqlsted=1}^\infty \left( \left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^{mfqlsted}-\frac{2}{6^{mfqlsted}} \right) \frac{5}{3} \left( \frac{2}{5} \right)^{mfqlsted} \\
&= \sum_{mfqlsted=1}^\infty \left( \frac{5}{3} \left(\frac{4}{15}\right)^{mfqlsted} - \frac{10}{3} \left(\frac{1}{15}\right)^{mfqlsted} \right) \\
&= \frac{85}{231}.
\end{align*}
Similarly,
\begin{align*}
lkjshdwe_2 &= \sum_{povndyex=1}^\infty \sum_{mfqlsted=povndyex+1}^\infty \frac{2^{mfqlsted+povndyex}-2^{mfqlsted-povndyex+1}}{3^{mfqlsted} 5^{povndyex}} \\
&= \sum_{povndyex=1}^\infty \left( \left( \left(\frac{2}{5}\right)^{povndyex}-\frac{2}{10^{povndyex}} \right) \sum_{mfqlsted=povndyex+1}^\infty \left(\frac{2}{3} \right)^{mfqlsted} \right) \\
&= \sum_{povndyex=1}^\infty \left( \left(\frac{2}{5}\right)^{povndyex}-\frac{2}{10^{povndyex}} \right) 3 \left( \frac{2}{3} \right)^{povndyex+1} \\
&= \sum_{povndyex=1}^\infty \left( 2 \left(\frac{4}{15}\right)^{povndyex} - 4 \left(\frac{1}{15}\right)^{povndyex} \right) \\
&= \frac{34}{77}.
\end{align*}
We conclude that $lkjshdwe = lkjshdwe_1+lkjshdwe_2 = \frac{17}{21}$.

\noindent
\textbf{Second solution:}
Recall that the real numbers $hjgrksla,mfqlsted,povndyex$ form the side lengths of a triangle if and only if
\[
kfgtrnma-hjgrksla, kfgtrnma-mfqlsted, kfgtrnma-povndyex > 0 \qquad kfgtrnma = \frac{hjgrksla+mfqlsted+povndyex}{2},
\]
and that if we put $wqpzokli = 2(kfgtrnma-hjgrksla), rnbcxwot = 2(kfgtrnma-mfqlsted), tolpqysa = 2(kfgtrnma-povndyex)$,
\[
hjgrksla = \frac{rnbcxwot+tolpqysa}{2}, mfqlsted = \frac{tolpqysa+wqpzokli}{2}, povndyex = \frac{wqpzokli+rnbcxwot}{2}.
\]
To generate all \emph{integer} triples $(hjgrksla,mfqlsted,povndyex)$ which form the side lengths of a triangle, we must also assume that $wqpzokli,rnbcxwot,tolpqysa$ are either all even or all odd. We may therefore write the original sum as
\[
%\sum_{(hjgrksla,mfqlsted,povndyex) \in qzxwvtnp} \frac{2^{hjgrksla}}{3^{mfqlsted} 5^{povndyex}}
%=
\sum_{wqpzokli,rnbcxwot,tolpqysa >0 \mbox{\small \,odd}} \frac{2^{(rnbcxwot+tolpqysa)/2}}{3^{(tolpqysa+wqpzokli)/2} 5^{(wqpzokli+rnbcxwot)/2}}
+ \sum_{wqpzokli,rnbcxwot,tolpqysa >0 \mbox{\small \,even}} \frac{2^{(rnbcxwot+tolpqysa)/2}}{3^{(tolpqysa+wqpzokli)/2} 5^{(wqpzokli+rnbcxwot)/2}}.
\]
To unify the two sums, we substitute in the first case  $wqpzokli = 2dfhgklwa+1, rnbcxwot = 2sqmnlrje+1, tolpqysa = 2gtrdseop+1$ and in the second case $wqpzokli = 2dfhgklwa+2, rnbcxwot = 2sqmnlrje+2, tolpqysa = 2gtrdseop+2$ to obtain
\begin{align*}
\sum_{(hjgrksla,mfqlsted,povndyex) \in qzxwvtnp} \frac{2^{hjgrksla}}{3^{mfqlsted} 5^{povndyex}}
&= \sum_{dfhgklwa,sqmnlrje,gtrdseop=1}^\infty \frac{2^{sqmnlrje+gtrdseop}}{3^{gtrdseop+dfhgklwa} 5^{dfhgklwa+sqmnlrje}} \left( 1 + \frac{2^{-1}}{3^{-1} 5^{-1}} \right) \\
&= \frac{17}{2} \sum_{dfhgklwa=1}^\infty \left( \frac{1}{15} \right)^{dfhgklwa} \sum_{sqmnlrje=1}^\infty
\left( \frac{2}{5} \right)^{sqmnlrje} \sum_{gtrdseop=1}^\infty \left( \frac{2}{3} \right)^{gtrdseop} \\
&= \frac{17}{2} \frac{1/15}{1-1/15} \frac{2/5}{1-2/5} \frac{2/3}{1-2/3} \\
&= \frac{17}{21}.
\end{align*}

kernel_variant.question:
Let  
 Q := {(a,b,c,d) \in  \mathbb{Z}_{>0}^4 : max{a,b,c,d} < \frac{1}{2}(a+b+c+d) and a+b+c+d is even}.    
(Thus the four positive integers are the side-lengths of at least one - not necessarily convex - quadrilateral, and the perimeter is even.)  

Evaluate, in lowest terms, the absolutely-convergent series  

  S = \sum _{(a,b,c,d)\in Q}  2^a /(3^b 5c 7^d).
kernel_variant.solution:
Step 1.  Half-perimeter parameters.  
Put s := \frac{1}{2}(a+b+c+d) and define  

 x := s-a, y := s-b, z := s-c, w := s-d.

Because max{a,b,c,d} < s, every x,y,z,w is a positive integer, and conversely  

 a = (y+z+w-x)/2, b = (x+z+w-y)/2, c = (x+y+w-z)/2, d = (x+y+z-w)/2.    (1)

Hence, with  

 (A1) x,y,z,w \in  \mathbb{Z}_{>0}; (A2) x+y+z+w even;   
 (A3) x<y+z+w, y<x+z+w, z<x+y+w, w<x+y+z,                                     (2)

one has  

 (a,b,c,d)\in Q \Leftrightarrow  (x,y,z,w) satisfies (A1)-(A3).                                 (3)

Temporarily we ignore (2) and work with  

 X := {(x,y,z,w)\in \mathbb{Z}_{>0}^4 : x+y+z+w even}.                                    (4)

Step 2.  Separating variables in the weight.  
Insert (1):

  2^a /(3^b5c7^d) = 2^{(y+z+w-x)/2} 3^{-(x+z+w-y)/2} 5^{-(x+y+w-z)/2} 7^{-(x+y+z-w)/2}  
                   = k_x^{\,x} k_y^{\,y} k_z^{\,z} k_w^{\,w},                    (5)

where  

 k_x := 1/\sqrt{210}, k_y := \sqrt{6/35}, k_z := \sqrt{10/21}, k_w := \sqrt{14/15}.          (6)

All four numbers lie strictly between 0 and 1, guaranteeing absolute convergence.

Step 3.  The parity filter.  
For 0<k<1,

 \sum _{m\geq 1, even} k^{m}=k^2/(1-k^2),  \sum _{m\geq 1, odd} k^{m}=k/(1-k^2).             (7)

Introduce  

 \Pi _even := (1+(-1)^{x+y+z+w})/2.                                               (8)

Thus  

 \Sigma _0 := \sum _{(x,y,z,w)\in X} k_x^{x}k_y^{y}k_z^{z}k_w^{w}  
      = \frac{1}{2}[(\sum _{x\geq 1}\pm )\cdots (\sum _{w\geq 1}\pm )]                                               
      = (k_xk_yk_zk_w)/2\cdot [(\prod (1-k_i)^{-1})+(\prod (1+k_i)^{-1})].                    (9)

Numerics.  
k_xk_yk_zk_w = 2/105.  
1-k_x^2=209/210, 1-k_y^2=29/35, 1-k_z^2=11/21, 1-k_w^2=1/15.  
Put P_\pm :=\prod (1\pm k_i).  Then  

 P_+P_- = \prod (1-k_i^2)=66 671/2 315 250, P_++P_- = 176/35.

Consequently  

 \Sigma _0 = (1/105)\cdot (P_++P_-)/(P_+P_-) = 10 080/6 061.                              (10)

Step 4.  Removing tuples that violate (2).  
The four inequalities in (2) are mutually exclusive, so we subtract their contributions one by one.

Take the ``x-dominant'' set (x \geq  y+z+w, even total).  Fix y,z,w \geq  1 and put t:=y+z+w.  
Then x runs through t,t+2,t+4,\ldots , giving  

 \sum _{x\geq t, x\equiv t (mod 2)} k_x^{x}=k_x^{t}/(1-k_x^2).

Hence the contribution of these tuples is  

  S_x^{bad}=\sum _{y,z,w\geq 1} k_y^{y}k_z^{z}k_w^{w}\cdot k_x^{y+z+w}/(1-k_x^2)  
           = k_x^3k_yk_zk_w                                                      (11)
              /[(1-k_x^2)(1-k_xk_y)(1-k_xk_z)(1-k_xk_w)],

because each outer geometric sum over y, z, w supplies a factor
 (1-k_xk_y)^{-1}, (1-k_xk_z)^{-1}, (1-k_xk_w)^{-1}, respectively.
(The crucial power k_x^{y+z+w} explains the k_x^3 in the numerator; this
was mis-printed in the earlier draft.)

With the constants (6):

 k_x^3k_yk_zk_w = 1/11 025,

 1-k_x^2 = 209/210, 1-k_xk_y = 34/35, 1-k_xk_z = 20/21, 1-k_xk_w = 14/15.

Plugging in and simplifying,

 S_x^{bad}=3/28 424.                                                           (12)

Cyclic permutation of (x,y,z,w) gives  

 S_y^{bad}=14/1 479, S_z^{bad}=21/275, S_w^{bad}=10/7.                        (13)

The four ``bad'' sets are disjoint, so no inclusion-exclusion corrections are required.

Step 5.  The final value.

 S = \Sigma _0 - (S_x^{bad}+S_y^{bad}+S_z^{bad}+S_w^{bad})  
   = 10 080/6 061 - (3/28 424 + 14/1 479 + 21/275 + 10/7)  
   = 10 609/71 400.                                                            (14)

Because gcd(10 609, 71 400)=1, the fraction is already in lowest terms.

Answer: S = 10 609 / 71 400.

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
•  Higher dimension: the problem passes from triples (triangle sides) to quadruples (quadrilateral sides), adding a whole extra variable and an extra inequality.  

•  Additional constraints: besides the quadrilateral inequality the perimeter is forced to be even, introducing a subtle parity condition that must be tracked throughout.  

•  More sophisticated structure: the solution needs a new parametrisation (x,y,z,w) that generalises the classical “Heron substitution’’ for triangles; proving its validity and handling parity doubles the case-work.  

•  Deeper theory: inclusion–exclusion is replaced by a factor-wise decomposition that uses four simultaneous geometric series and careful square-root algebra; every factor carries a different base, so cancellations are far less obvious than in the triangular case.  

•  Many interacting concepts: the argument blends inequality characterisation, parity analysis, algebraic manipulation of square roots, and exact rational simplification, all in four independent sums.  Each step is strictly more involved than in the original problem, and no single observation or pattern matching can short-cut the computation—one must work through all layers to reach the final reduced fraction 107/1190.

original_kernel_variant.question:
Let  
 Q := {(a,b,c,d) \in  \mathbb{Z}_{>0}^4 : max{a,b,c,d} < \frac{1}{2}(a+b+c+d) and a+b+c+d is even}.    
(Thus the four positive integers are the side-lengths of at least one - not necessarily convex - quadrilateral, and the perimeter is even.)  

Evaluate, in lowest terms, the absolutely-convergent series  

  S = \sum _{(a,b,c,d)\in Q}  2^a /(3^b 5c 7^d).
original_kernel_variant.solution:
Step 1.  Half-perimeter parameters.  
Put s := \frac{1}{2}(a+b+c+d) and define  

 x := s-a, y := s-b, z := s-c, w := s-d.

Because max{a,b,c,d} < s, every x,y,z,w is a positive integer, and conversely  

 a = (y+z+w-x)/2, b = (x+z+w-y)/2, c = (x+y+w-z)/2, d = (x+y+z-w)/2.    (1)

Hence, with  

 (A1) x,y,z,w \in  \mathbb{Z}_{>0}; (A2) x+y+z+w even;   
 (A3) x<y+z+w, y<x+z+w, z<x+y+w, w<x+y+z,                                     (2)

one has  

 (a,b,c,d)\in Q \Leftrightarrow  (x,y,z,w) satisfies (A1)-(A3).                                 (3)

Temporarily we ignore (2) and work with  

 X := {(x,y,z,w)\in \mathbb{Z}_{>0}^4 : x+y+z+w even}.                                    (4)

Step 2.  Separating variables in the weight.  
Insert (1):

  2^a /(3^b5c7^d) = 2^{(y+z+w-x)/2} 3^{-(x+z+w-y)/2} 5^{-(x+y+w-z)/2} 7^{-(x+y+z-w)/2}  
                   = k_x^{\,x} k_y^{\,y} k_z^{\,z} k_w^{\,w},                    (5)

where  

 k_x := 1/\sqrt{210}, k_y := \sqrt{6/35}, k_z := \sqrt{10/21}, k_w := \sqrt{14/15}.          (6)

All four numbers lie strictly between 0 and 1, guaranteeing absolute convergence.

Step 3.  The parity filter.  
For 0<k<1,

 \sum _{m\geq 1, even} k^{m}=k^2/(1-k^2),  \sum _{m\geq 1, odd} k^{m}=k/(1-k^2).             (7)

Introduce  

 \Pi _even := (1+(-1)^{x+y+z+w})/2.                                               (8)

Thus  

 \Sigma _0 := \sum _{(x,y,z,w)\in X} k_x^{x}k_y^{y}k_z^{z}k_w^{w}  
      = \frac{1}{2}[(\sum _{x\geq 1}\pm )\cdots (\sum _{w\geq 1}\pm )]                                               
      = (k_xk_yk_zk_w)/2\cdot [(\prod (1-k_i)^{-1})+(\prod (1+k_i)^{-1})].                    (9)

Numerics.  
k_xk_yk_zk_w = 2/105.  
1-k_x^2=209/210, 1-k_y^2=29/35, 1-k_z^2=11/21, 1-k_w^2=1/15.  
Put P_\pm :=\prod (1\pm k_i).  Then  

 P_+P_- = \prod (1-k_i^2)=66 671/2 315 250, P_++P_- = 176/35.

Consequently  

 \Sigma _0 = (1/105)\cdot (P_++P_-)/(P_+P_-) = 10 080/6 061.                              (10)

Step 4.  Removing tuples that violate (2).  
The four inequalities in (2) are mutually exclusive, so we subtract their contributions one by one.

Take the ``x-dominant'' set (x \geq  y+z+w, even total).  Fix y,z,w \geq  1 and put t:=y+z+w.  
Then x runs through t,t+2,t+4,\ldots , giving  

 \sum _{x\geq t, x\equiv t (mod 2)} k_x^{x}=k_x^{t}/(1-k_x^2).

Hence the contribution of these tuples is  

  S_x^{bad}=\sum _{y,z,w\geq 1} k_y^{y}k_z^{z}k_w^{w}\cdot k_x^{y+z+w}/(1-k_x^2)  
           = k_x^3k_yk_zk_w                                                      (11)
              /[(1-k_x^2)(1-k_xk_y)(1-k_xk_z)(1-k_xk_w)],

because each outer geometric sum over y, z, w supplies a factor
 (1-k_xk_y)^{-1}, (1-k_xk_z)^{-1}, (1-k_xk_w)^{-1}, respectively.
(The crucial power k_x^{y+z+w} explains the k_x^3 in the numerator; this
was mis-printed in the earlier draft.)

With the constants (6):

 k_x^3k_yk_zk_w = 1/11 025,

 1-k_x^2 = 209/210, 1-k_xk_y = 34/35, 1-k_xk_z = 20/21, 1-k_xk_w = 14/15.

Plugging in and simplifying,

 S_x^{bad}=3/28 424.                                                           (12)

Cyclic permutation of (x,y,z,w) gives  

 S_y^{bad}=14/1 479, S_z^{bad}=21/275, S_w^{bad}=10/7.                        (13)

The four ``bad'' sets are disjoint, so no inclusion-exclusion corrections are required.

Step 5.  The final value.

 S = \Sigma _0 - (S_x^{bad}+S_y^{bad}+S_z^{bad}+S_w^{bad})  
   = 10 080/6 061 - (3/28 424 + 14/1 479 + 21/275 + 10/7)  
   = 10 609/71 400.                                                            (14)

Because gcd(10 609, 71 400)=1, the fraction is already in lowest terms.

Answer: S = 10 609 / 71 400.
